texts.full_text: re's the breakdown: 1. Protocol Definition: - The protocol `<NSObject>` means it inherits from NSObject. - It defines two required methods: `initiatingPayload:` and `isComplete`. 2. Method Definitions: - `- (NSData )arg1;`: This method returns an NSData objecT, which could be some initial payload data used to start a process or operation. The parameter `(id*)` suggests it's expecting a pointer to any type of argument. - `- (bool)isComplete;`: Returns a boolean indicating whether the associated process is complete. 3. Incomplete Code: - The third line appears incomplete (`@required - (NSData...`). It might have been cut off, but if this were part of a full protocol definition, there would likely be more method signatures following `@required`. ### Usage Context This protocol could be used in scenarios where you need to manage some kind of circular or iterative process that involves keychain operations. For example: - Keychain Operations: It might be related to securely handling credentials or tokens. - Circular Buffers/Processes: Managing a buffer or state that cycles through data. ### Example Implementation If

texts.full_text: so that the resources are not over-allocated. (Christianson) The CUPs are periodically reviewed and the most recent information is used in the renewal process. (Hamann) The problem is that although the CUPs are only for a limited duration, people have an expectation that they will continue to be able to use that amount of water at a certain cost. A big problem is that we are realizing that we have over-allocated the resources and that the natural systems are becoming stressed and then informing the land owners and local governments that their expectations of water use must change. (Christianson) Is this a structural or a performance problem? (Tschinkel) To the degree that data are not adopted by rule, the WMD should be more aggressive in this area, but the local governments must react to the information and its consequences. Another structural problem has been that some WMDs have not conserved a portion of the resource during low flow periods. texts.full_text: alysis of non-small cell lung cancer using array comparative genomic hybridization: implications of the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase pathway. Genomic abnormalities at 348 loci encoding genes that may contribute to lung cancer transformation and progression were assessed using array comparative genomic hybridization in 21 squamous carcinomas (SqCas) and 16 adenocarcinomas (AdCas). Hierarchical clustering showed a clear pattern of gains and losses for the SqCas, whereas the pattern for AdCas was less distinct. Cross-validated classification using a K-nearest-neighbor assigned, on average, 32 of 37 samples to their proper histological subtype. The most noticeable differences between SqCas and AdCas were gain of c...of cyclin D1 (CCND1) at locus rs10522272 in the case-control group versus a loss-of-function variant associated with increased risk, suggesting that CCND1 may play an important role as oncogene. The most striking difference between SqCa and AdC was gain of c-MET exon 14 skipping mutations which were found to be significantly more frequent among patients who received chemotherapy compared those on targeted therapy (p=0 .05)

texts.preview_tail: nclusive verdict. The murder of Sunil Joshi serves as a reminder of the complex and often violent history of India's religious and political landscape. It highlights the deep-seated divisions and conf
texts.full_text: Hindutva idealogue in Malwa region, was shot dead in Dewas on December 29, 2007. The Madhya Pradesh police had stopped investigation when they found that leaders of BJP and Sangh Parivar may get exposed in the probe. It was an inside job. There are various theories. The first linked to Congress leader Pyar Singh Ninama, who had cut Joshi's 'choti' (tuft of hair) publicly in Mhow. Joshi had left Mhow and shifted to Dewas where he lived with four RSS workers in a house. Later Ninama was murdered. Later Ninama's son was also killed. Following the tribal leader's killing, Joshi was shot dead and three bullets pumped in his body. There was theory that he was bragging about his role in organising the terrorist attacks and this scared others who feared that he would expose them also. Shrikant Purohit had allegedly said that it was Joshi who had been behind the Samjhauta Express bomb strike. But police closed the case and the investigation including the last calls made on his cellphone, the people who visited him in his final days, and the circumstances surrounding his death remained largely unresolved. Joshi's murder remains a contentious issue within India, with various factions pointing fingers at each other. Some believe that Joshi was silenced to prevent him from revealing information about the involvement of RSS members in terrorist activities. Others argue that political rivalry played a significant role in his death, as he was seen as a key figure in the Hindutva movement. The case has been reopened several times over the years, with new evidence and testimonies surfacing periodically. However, despite these efforts, the truth behind Joshi's murder remains elusive. The investigation has been complicated by the involvement of high-profile individuals and political parties, making it difficult to arrive at a conclusive verdict. The murder of Sunil Joshi serves as a reminder of the complex and often violent history of India's religious and political landscape. It highlights the deep-seated divisions and conf
